h1: How IN-SPACe Authorisation Works in India: Procedure, Timelines, and Documents
meta_title: IN-SPACe Authorisation in India: Full Procedure Guide
meta_description: Complete guide to IN-SPACe authorisation: NGP 2024 activity categories, 75 to 120 day timeline, documents, WPC, AERB, FDI rules, and foreign entity routes.
tldr: IN-SPACe authorisation is the permission granted by the Indian National Space Promotion and Authorisation Centre, under the Department of Space, that any non-government entity must obtain to conduct space activities to or from Indian territory. Issued under the Norms, Guidelines and Procedures notified in May 2024, the framework covers satellites, launch vehicles, ground stations, re-entry, and earth observation data dissemination, with statutory processing of 75 to 120 days after a complete application.
canonical_summary:
IN-SPACe authorisation is the operative permission for private space activity in India. Established in June 2020 and given an authorisation mandate by the Indian Space Policy 2023, IN-SPACe acts as the single-window regulator under the Department of Space. The detailed framework lives in the Norms, Guidelines and Procedures for Implementation of the Indian Space Policy 2023 (NGP), reference IN:ISP2023:NGP2024/V1.0, released on 3 May 2024.

The NGP defines six families of activities that require authorisation, sets a target processing window of 75 to 120 days from a complete application, restricts applicants to Indian entities, and routes foreign operators through an Indian subsidiary, joint venture, or recognised collaboration. Authorisation is granted for the establishment and operation of satellites and constellations in GSO and NGSO, orbital and sub-orbital launches, ground systems, planned re-entry, and dissemination of high-resolution earth observation data with ground sampling distance of 30 cm or finer.

The authorisation is the central permission but not the only one. Operators interact with the Wireless Planning and Coordination Wing of the Department of Telecommunications for spectrum and ITU filing, with the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board for any radioactive payload, and with Indian Customs to claim the duty exemption for satellites, payloads, and launch hardware. The 2024 amendment to the FDI policy on the space sector, notified by the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade on 4 March 2024, layered a new investment regime on top: 74 percent automatic for satellite manufacturing and operation, 49 percent automatic for launch vehicles and spaceports, and 100 percent automatic for sub-components.

sections.heading: What IN-SPACe authorisation is and who needs it
paragraphs: IN-SPACe, the Indian National Space Promotion and Authorisation Centre, is an autonomous agency under the Department of Space, Government of India. It was set up in June 2020 as part of the space-sector reforms that opened private participation. Under the Indian Space Policy 2023, notified on 6 April 2023 by the Union Cabinet, IN-SPACe is the single-window regulator that authorises, promotes, and supervises every non-government space activity carried out to or from Indian territory.

Authorisation is required of any entity that proposes to operate a satellite or constellation in geostationary (GSO) or non-geostationary (NGSO) orbit, launch a vehicle from or land one in India, run a ground station for telemetry tracking and command (TT&C) or for earth observation reception, disseminate high-resolution remote sensing data, host a payload on someone else's bus, conduct in-orbit servicing, or transfer ownership or control of a space object already in orbit. The IN-SPACe FAQ clarifies that pure hardware manufacturing and supply, ground qualification testing, and end-use consumption of satellite capacity or imagery do not need authorisation.

The applicant universe spans Indian launch service providers such as Skyroot Aerospace and Agnikul Cosmos, earth observation operators including Pixxel, Dhruva Space, KaleidEO (a SatSure subsidiary), and the new private L-band SAR operator Sisir Radar, satellite-service consumers buying capacity from foreign constellations, and propulsion and platform vendors like Bellatrix Aerospace. Foreign operators, for example Starlink-type NGSO operators or established GEO fleets seeking to sell capacity in India, must route through an Indian entity.
bullets: Satellite establishment and operation, including GSO, NGSO, communication, remote sensing, hosted payloads, amateur and scientific missions
Orbital and sub-orbital launches from or to Indian territory
Planned re-entry of space objects, with or without recovery
Ground systems including satellite control centres, TT&C, mission control centres, and remote sensing data reception
Dissemination of remote sensing data finer than 30 cm GSD over Indian territory
Sale or transfer of a space object in orbit or change of operational control

sections.heading: The application flow end to end
paragraphs: The process runs on the IN-SPACe Digital Platform, the paperless portal that has been the official submission channel since August 2022. The end-to-end flow has three logical stages: preliminary engagement, formal application, and grant.

Preliminary engagement is the optional but recommended pre-filing step. The applicant approaches IN-SPACe through the Promotion Directorate, presents the mission concept, and receives feedback on which authorisation category applies, what documents will be needed, and which other regulators will be consulted. In practice, every serious applicant uses this stage to scope spectrum coordination with WPC and ITU filing dependencies before paying the application fee.

Formal application is submitted on the IDP with the prescribed forms, the engineering data package, and supporting attachments. Fees are paid through the Bharatkosh portal of the Government of India. IN-SPACe acknowledges receipt, conducts a preliminary completeness check, and then circulates the application for interministerial and interdepartmental consultation, typically with the Department of Telecommunications (WPC), the Department of Space units, the National Security Council Secretariat for security clearance, and where relevant the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board, the Directorate General of Civil Aviation, and the Ministry of External Affairs for foreign launch arrangements.

Grant or rejection issues at the end of the consultation cycle. The NGP commits to a 75 to 120 day processing window from receipt of a complete application. The authorisation letter specifies the mission scope, validity period, operational conditions, frequency assignments, debris mitigation milestones, and reporting obligations. Most operators receive conditional or phased authorisations that mature as design reviews, launch readiness, and in-orbit checkout are cleared.

sections.heading: Activity categories under the NGP 2024 framework
paragraphs: The Norms, Guidelines and Procedures document IN:ISP2023:NGP2024/V1.0 organises authorisations by activity category rather than by a simple risk tier. The 147-page text was released on 3 May 2024 and operationalises the Indian Space Policy 2023.

Each category carries its own annex of technical criteria, eligibility tests, and conditions. The satellite category, for instance, separates communication satellites, earth observation satellites, hosted payloads, and other scientific or amateur missions. The launch category separates sub-orbital from orbital, and Indian-soil launches from foreign-soil launches by Indian entities. The data dissemination category turns on the 30 cm ground sampling distance threshold, below which any commercial transaction in primary data pertaining to Indian territory must run through an IN-SPACe registered data disseminator.

While the NGP does not use the words Class A, B, or C in the way that some other jurisdictions do, the IN-SPACe FAQ and practice differentiate the depth of scrutiny by mission risk profile. Communication constellations with cross-border service implications, large launchers, and high-resolution imaging missions undergo the deepest review, including security and dual-use evaluation. Hosted payloads on certified Indian buses and low-risk experimental sub-orbital launches receive a lighter touch.
bullets: Satellites and constellations: GSO and NGSO communication, remote sensing, hosted payloads, amateur, scientific, technology demonstration
Launch vehicles: orbital and sub-orbital launches from Indian territory or from outside India by Indian entities
Planned re-entry: controlled disposal, returnable capsules, and recovery operations
Ground systems: SCC, MCC, TT&C, RSDC, and Ground Station as a Service (GSaaS) for Indian and foreign clients
Data dissemination: high-resolution earth observation data finer than 30 cm GSD over Indian territory
In-orbit services and transfer of operational control of space objects

sections.heading: Document and engineering data requirements
paragraphs: The NGP's annexures specify the engineering data each category must submit. The objective is to let IN-SPACe verify managerial, technical, infrastructural, financial, and operational capability and to confirm that the mission will not violate India's international obligations under the Outer Space Treaty, the Liability Convention, the Registration Convention, the ITU Radio Regulations, and the UN COPUOS debris mitigation guidelines.

For a satellite mission, the application typically must include the orbital parameter set (semi-major axis, inclination, eccentricity, mean anomaly, RAAN, argument of perigee), the manoeuvre and station-keeping plan, the propulsion budget, the collision avoidance and conjunction screening process, the debris mitigation plan covering passivation and end-of-life disposal consistent with the 25-year rule (or the current IN-SPACe-aligned target), the radiation and reliability analysis, the launch and early operations plan, and the in-orbit anomaly and contingency procedures.

For a launch vehicle, the package extends to the vehicle configuration and stage-wise data, the trajectory and range safety analysis, the flight termination system, the ground hazard analysis, the third-party liability insurance cover, and the launch site authorisation tie-ins with the Satish Dhawan Space Centre or any private spaceport. Agnikul Cosmos's Dhanush launchpad and the Skyroot launch infrastructure inside Sriharikota are precedents the IN-SPACe range safety reviewers benchmark against.

Every application carries a security plan. For communication satellites this includes lawful interception and monitoring obligations, gateway location, encryption keys handling, and compliance with the rules under the Telegraph Act for spectrum use. For earth observation it includes data masking for sensitive Indian sites, shutter control over restricted areas, and disclosure procedures for foreign customer access.

sections.heading: Timelines, fees, and validity
paragraphs: The NGP commits to a 75 to 120 day decision window from receipt of a complete application. Applicants are advised to file at least six months before they need the authorisation, because the clock pauses every time IN-SPACe asks for clarifications and only restarts on a complete response.

Fees are paid through the Bharatkosh portal. The IN-SPACe fee schedule lists separate heads for application processing, authorisation, renewal, and amendment. Fees scale with mission category and size, with launch vehicle and large constellation authorisations attracting higher charges than single small satellites or hosted payloads.

Authorisations are typically issued for the operational life of the mission, with checkpoints at preliminary design review, critical design review, launch readiness, and in-orbit acceptance. They are not transferable without IN-SPACe consent, and any change in ownership, control, mission profile, frequency, or orbital slot triggers an amendment application.

Between November 2022 and December 2024, IN-SPACe granted 38 authorisations to 23 different non-government entities, a useful proxy for the steady throughput of the office. Most authorisations in that period were granted to foreign satellite operators provisioning capacity into India, with a smaller but growing share for Indian-owned satellite operations and launches.

sections.heading: Interface with WPC and the Department of Telecommunications for spectrum
paragraphs: Every communication satellite or ground station application has a parallel track at the Wireless Planning and Coordination Wing of the Department of Telecommunications. WPC is the national radio regulatory authority and holds the responsibility under the Indian Telegraph Act and the Indian Wireless Telegraphy Act for frequency assignment, the National Frequency Allocation Plan, and India's filings at the International Telecommunication Union.

An IN-SPACe authorisation cannot stand on its own for a communication service. The applicant needs a corresponding ITU filing by India through WPC, a Standing Advisory Committee on Frequency Allocation (SACFA) clearance for any earth station, and the relevant telecommunication licence such as a GMPCS authorisation for satellite gateway services or an ISP licence for ground-segment retail. IN-SPACe coordinates with WPC, the World Meteorological Organisation interface for science bands, and the Network Operations Control Centre for harmful interference, and it retains the right to direct an operator to cease emissions if interference is not resolved within the notice period.

Foreign satellite operators that wish to provide capacity in India have been on a hard deadline: from 1 April 2025, only those non-Indian satellites with IN-SPACe authorisation may provision satellite communication services within India. This effectively forces every foreign GSO or NGSO operator selling into India to complete the authorisation, ITU coordination, and licensing trifecta before they can sign Indian customers.

sections.heading: Interface with AERB, customs, and other allied regulators
paragraphs: Any mission carrying a radioactive payload, for example a radioisotope thermoelectric generator or radioisotope heater unit, requires consent from the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board under the Atomic Energy Act 1962. The AERB issues consents in the form of Licence, Authorisation, Registration, or Approval depending on the radiation source and category. IN-SPACe will not clear a mission with a nuclear power source until AERB has issued the relevant consent, and the safety and contingency planning around launch and re-entry must be consistent with the UN Principles Relevant to the Use of Nuclear Power Sources in Outer Space.

Customs is the other constant interface. Indian customs has long offered an exemption from basic customs duty and integrated GST for the import of satellites and payloads (including ground equipment) and for the scientific and technical instruments, parts, raw materials, and consumables required for launch vehicles and satellites. The exemption sits under Section 25(1) of the Customs Act 1962 and has been consolidated in 2025 by Notification No. 45/2025-Customs, which supersedes more than thirty earlier notifications and ties space-sector entries to end-use certification.

Other touchpoints include the Directorate General of Civil Aviation for airspace clearance during launches, the Ministry of External Affairs for the registration of space objects with the United Nations under the Registration Convention, the Ministry of Home Affairs for security clearance of foreign-linked applicants, and customs and DGFT for the import of dual-use components controlled under the SCOMET list.

sections.heading: Foreign entity participation and the 2024 FDI regime
paragraphs: Only an Indian entity can apply to IN-SPACe. A non-Indian operator must enter India through an Indian subsidiary, a joint venture with an Indian entity, or an authorised representative arrangement recognised by the Government. The Indian vehicle is the applicant and licensee of record, and the foreign parent's data, technology, and operational role are described in the application.

The Union Cabinet amended the FDI policy on the space sector on 21 February 2024, and the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade notified Press Note No. 1 of 2024 dated 4 March 2024 to give it effect. The new regime layered investment thresholds onto the IN-SPACe authorisation regime. For satellite manufacturing and operation, satellite data products, and ground and user segments, foreign investment up to 74 percent is permitted under the automatic route, with anything beyond 74 percent and up to 100 percent under the government route. For launch vehicles, associated systems and subsystems, and the creation of spaceports, the automatic route covers up to 49 percent, with the government route above. For the manufacture of components and sub-systems for satellites, ground segment and user segment, the entire 100 percent is automatic.

The legislative ceiling on the regime remains the draft Space Activities Bill. IN-SPACe Chairperson Pawan Goenka confirmed in May 2025 that the redrafted bill is in interministerial consultation and is expected to give IN-SPACe statutory authority and a long-arm jurisdiction over Indian nationals conducting space activities abroad, replacing today's executive arrangement.

faqs.question: What does IN-SPACe stand for and where does it sit in the Government of India?
faqs.answer: IN-SPACe stands for the Indian National Space Promotion and Authorisation Centre. It was set up in June 2020 as an autonomous body under the Department of Space, Government of India, headquartered in Ahmedabad. It is the single-window regulator that authorises, promotes, and supervises space activities by non-government entities. Today it derives its mandate from the Indian Space Policy 2023, notified on 6 April 2023, and from the Norms, Guidelines and Procedures (NGP) document released on 3 May 2024. A draft Space Activities Bill, in interministerial consultation as of 2025, is expected to convert this executive mandate into statutory authority.

faqs.question: Which activities actually require IN-SPACe authorisation?
faqs.answer: The NGP lists six families of activities that require authorisation: establishment and operation of satellites and constellations in GSO or NGSO for communication, earth observation, hosted payloads, amateur and scientific use; sub-orbital and orbital launches from or to Indian territory; planned re-entry of space objects; establishment and operation of ground systems including satellite control centres, TT&C and remote sensing data reception; dissemination of high-resolution remote sensing data finer than 30 cm GSD over Indian territory; and sale or transfer of operational control of an in-orbit space object. Hardware manufacturing, ground qualification testing, and end-user consumption of satellite capacity do not need authorisation.

faqs.question: How long does an IN-SPACe authorisation take from filing to grant?
faqs.answer: The NGP commits to a target window of 75 to 120 days from receipt of a complete application. The clock starts only when IN-SPACe has confirmed completeness and pauses when clarifications are pending. The official advisory is to file at least six months before the date you need the authorisation, because interministerial consultations with the Department of Telecommunications, the National Security Council Secretariat, and any other touched regulator can extend the calendar time well beyond the 120 days. For very large constellations or sensitive missions, applicants should plan for an even longer horizon.

faqs.question: Can a foreign company apply for IN-SPACe authorisation directly?
faqs.answer: No. The NGP restricts applicants to Indian entities. A foreign operator can enter the Indian regime through an Indian subsidiary, a joint venture with an Indian partner, or any other commercial collaboration recognised by the Government, such as an authorised representative arrangement. The Indian vehicle is the applicant of record and holds the licence. The application discloses the foreign parent's role, technology, and data flows. From 1 April 2025, foreign satellite operators selling capacity in India must hold an IN-SPACe authorisation through such an Indian vehicle.

faqs.question: What does the 2024 FDI policy mean for foreign investors in Indian space companies?
faqs.answer: Following the Cabinet decision of 21 February 2024 and Press Note 1 of 2024 dated 4 March 2024, foreign direct investment in the Indian space sector is tiered. Satellite manufacturing and operation, satellite data products, and ground and user segment activities allow up to 74 percent FDI under the automatic route, with anything above under the government route. Launch vehicles, associated systems, and spaceports allow up to 49 percent automatic. Components and sub-systems for satellites, ground and user segment can be 100 percent automatic. FDI thresholds are separate from, and additional to, the IN-SPACe authorisation.

faqs.question: What engineering documents are required for a satellite authorisation application?
faqs.answer: A satellite application typically must include the orbital parameter set and station-keeping plan, the propulsion and manoeuvre budget, the collision avoidance and conjunction management process, the debris mitigation plan covering passivation and end-of-life disposal, the radiation and reliability analysis, the launch and early operations plan, the in-orbit anomaly procedures, the frequency plan with the linked ITU filing reference, the ground segment description, the third-party liability insurance cover, and a security plan covering lawful interception, gateway location, and data handling. Earth observation missions add a shutter control and data masking plan over sensitive Indian sites.

faqs.question: How does the spectrum and ITU filing process interact with IN-SPACe?
faqs.answer: Spectrum is regulated by the Wireless Planning and Coordination Wing of the Department of Telecommunications under the Indian Telegraph Act 1885 and the Indian Wireless Telegraphy Act 1933. WPC manages the National Frequency Allocation Plan and India's filings at the International Telecommunication Union. An IN-SPACe authorisation is granted in coordination with WPC, but does not itself confer spectrum rights. Applicants must also obtain SACFA clearance for earth stations and the relevant telecom service licence, such as GMPCS for satellite gateway services. IN-SPACe and WPC have stop-emission powers if a satellite causes harmful interference.

faqs.question: When is the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board involved in an IN-SPACe authorisation?
faqs.answer: AERB is involved whenever a mission carries a radioactive source, such as a radioisotope thermoelectric generator, a radioisotope heater unit, or any other licensed radiation source. AERB issues regulatory consent under the Atomic Energy Act 1962 in the form of Licence, Authorisation, Registration, or Approval. IN-SPACe will not finalise an authorisation for a mission with a nuclear power source until AERB has issued the relevant consent, and the application must show consistency with the UN Principles Relevant to the Use of Nuclear Power Sources in Outer Space.

faqs.question: What is the customs duty exemption for satellites and payloads imported into India?
faqs.answer: India provides exemption from basic customs duty and integrated GST on the import of satellites and payloads (including ground equipment) and of the scientific and technical instruments, apparatus, parts, components, spares, tools, mock-ups, modules, raw materials, and consumables required for launch vehicles and satellites. This exemption sits under Section 25(1) of the Customs Act 1962. The Ministry of Finance issued Notification No. 45/2025-Customs to consolidate over thirty older notifications into a single rationalised framework, tying space sector entries to end-use certification.

faqs.question: Do I need IN-SPACe authorisation to operate a ground station in India for a foreign satellite?
faqs.answer: Yes. Establishment and operation of ground systems, including TT&C, satellite control centres, mission control centres, and remote sensing data reception, are explicitly listed in the NGP as authorisation-required activities. Dhruva Space and Azista Aerospace have received IN-SPACe authorisations to offer Ground Station as a Service in UHF, VHF, S, and X bands to both Indian and foreign clients. The ground station authorisation runs in parallel with WPC's SACFA clearance for the radio equipment and the relevant telecom licence for the service offered.

faqs.question: Is hosted payload activity treated the same as owning a satellite?
faqs.answer: No. The NGP separates hosted payloads from full satellite ownership. A hosted payload provider supplies a payload that flies on someone else's bus, often a foreign GEO operator or an Indian launch vehicle's secondary deck. The hosted payload provider must still obtain IN-SPACe authorisation, but the scope of review is narrower and concentrates on the payload's operational concept, frequency and data plan, security plan, and on confirmation that the host satellite operator carries the orbital, debris, and liability responsibilities. The host operator must also be IN-SPACe authorised if the service touches Indian territory.

faqs.question: What happens if I sell, transfer, or change control of an in-orbit space object?
faqs.answer: Any sale, transfer of ownership, or change of operational control of a space object already in orbit is itself an authorisation-required activity under the NGP. The new operator, or the new controlling entity, must apply to IN-SPACe with the updated mission profile, security plan, and frequency arrangements. India retains its obligations under the Registration Convention and the Liability Convention for objects on its national registry, so the Ministry of External Affairs is consulted whenever a transfer would change the launching state representation at the United Nations Office for Outer Space Affairs.

faqs.question: Is there a fee schedule and how do I pay?
faqs.answer: Yes. The IN-SPACe fee schedule lists separate fees for application processing, authorisation, renewal, and amendment. Fees scale with the activity category and mission size, with launch vehicles and large constellations attracting higher fees than small satellites or hosted payloads. Payments are made through the Bharatkosh portal of the Controller General of Accounts, Government of India. The IN-SPACe Digital Platform generates a payment reference at the time the application is filed, and the receipt is uploaded back to the IDP to complete the submission.

faqs.question: Will the draft Space Activities Bill change any of this?
faqs.answer: Yes, but not immediately. IN-SPACe Chairperson Pawan Goenka confirmed in May 2025 that a redrafted Space Activities Bill is in interministerial consultation, eight years after the 2017 draft. The new bill is expected to give IN-SPACe statutory authority, formalise long-arm jurisdiction over Indian nationals conducting space activities abroad, set up a clearer liability and insurance regime, and reform the intellectual property treatment that drew industry criticism in 2017. Until the bill is enacted, IN-SPACe operates under the executive framework of the Indian Space Policy 2023 and the NGP.

faqs.question: What is the typical end-to-end timeline for an Indian launch service provider to operate?
faqs.answer: An Indian launch operator like Skyroot Aerospace or Agnikul Cosmos typically goes through three IN-SPACe milestones. The first is the launch vehicle development authorisation, granted on the basis of technical, financial, and security capability. The second is the launch-specific authorisation, granted ahead of each flight after a launch readiness review with the range safety and trajectory analysis cleared. The third is a continuing operations regime for repeated launches from Sriharikota or any private spaceport. Foreign payloads on Indian rockets require independent IN-SPACe clearance for the launch service contract.
